metadata.summary: DDD Aggregate root pattern: cluster entities under one root, enforce invariants via intention-revealing methods, raise domain events, never use public setters.

content.title: DDD Aggregates: Invariant-Enforcing Cluster Roots
content.summary: An Aggregate is a cluster of domain objects (Entities + Value Objects) treated as a single unit for data changes. One Entity is designated the Aggregate Root: all external access goes through the root, and all business invariants are enforced inside the root's methods. No public setters; use intention-revealing command methods (order.place(), order.cancel()) that validate state before mutating it.

why.p: Anemic domain models push business rules into Service classes that manipulate entity fields directly. The result: invariants are enforced only when callers remember to call the right service. The DDD Aggregate pattern moves invariants inside the entity, making them impossible to bypass: if order.place() is the only way to set status=PLACED, the "order must have lines before it can be placed" rule is always enforced, by construction.

The Aggregate boundary also defines the transaction scope: one Aggregate per DB transaction. Cross-Aggregate consistency is eventual, coordinated via Domain Events. This prevents both under-locking (missing invariant checks) and over-locking (long transactions spanning unrelated state).

list.item: Any domain object with lifecycle state and business invariants (Order, Subscription, Account, Booking).
When multiple entities must change together atomically (Order + its OrderLines).
When you need to record that something happened (Domain Events) as part of a state transition.
Complex domains with multiple teams: the Aggregate root is the API contract for the cluster.
Pure CRUD entities with no invariants — a plain ORM model with getters/setters is simpler and faster.
Read-only projections or query models — Aggregates are for the write side; read models use flat DTOs.
ETL / batch transforms where there is no transactional domain logic.

rule.statement: The Aggregate Root MUST be the only entry point for mutations: external code MUST NOT reach into child Entities or Value Objects and mutate them directly. All state changes happen through named command methods on the root.
rule.rationale: Direct mutation bypasses invariant checks. Intention-revealing methods (place, cancel, ship) document the domain vocabulary and enforce pre-conditions before mutating state.

rule.statement: Each Aggregate Root method that changes state MUST raise a Domain Event (appended to an internal _events list) describing what happened. The application layer collects and dispatches events after the DB transaction commits.
rule.rationale: Events decouple downstream reactions (email, inventory reserve, billing) from the Aggregate. Collecting before dispatch ensures events are only raised when the transaction succeeds.

rule.statement: Aggregates MUST NOT hold direct object references to other Aggregate Roots. Reference by identity (UUID) only. Cross-Aggregate state access goes through Repositories or Domain Services.
rule.rationale: Object references create implicit coupling and allow bypass of the other Aggregate's invariants. Identity references keep transaction boundaries clean and enable eventual consistency across Aggregates.

rule.statement: Aggregate invariants MUST be expressed as failing unit tests before implementation. Each invariant becomes a test: test_cannot_place_empty_order, test_cannot_ship_unpaid_order, test_cannot_modify_placed_order.
rule.rationale: Tests pin the invariants as living documentation and catch regressions. Writing tests first forces explicit invariant identification before coding begins.

example.title: Order Aggregate root with invariants and events
example.note: No public setters. Every state change goes through a named method that validates pre-conditions and raises an event. collect_events() clears the buffer after dispatch.

example.title: Anemic model — invariants in service, not entity
example.note: Any caller can set order.status directly. Invariants are enforced only when OrderService.place_order is called — bypass is trivial.

example.title: Crossing aggregate boundaries
example.note: The application service loads both Aggregates and coordinates. Each Aggregate is responsible only for its own invariants.

section.name: invariant-tests
section.p: Write invariant tests before writing Aggregate methods. Each invariant becomes one test. These tests are the living specification of the Aggregate's rules.

example.title: Aggregate invariant tests (pytest)

section.name: agentic-gotchas
section.p: AI agents produce anemic models by default — entities become dataclasses with services manipulating fields. Pin these instructions in the agent prompt and review for violations.

list.item: Pin "no public setters; behavior on the entity" — agents default to getter/setter patterns from training data.
Flag any cross-Aggregate field access (order.customer.wallet.balance) — agents traverse object graphs freely.
Require explicit naming: Domain Services hold pure logic; Application Services orchestrate (transaction + repo + dispatch). Agents conflate the two.
Watch for "DDD washing": adding domain/, application/, infrastructure/ folder names while keeping anemic models is theater, not DDD.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: DDD Aggregate work splits into modeling (high-judgment) and implementation (mechanical). Use a planner subagent (Opus) to produce a Bounded Context map and Aggregate diagrams as a checkpoint for human review. Once the model is signed off, an implementation subagent (Sonnet/Haiku) generates Aggregate roots from the spec.
section.prompt: Implement Subscription aggregate per locked spec at .product/ddd/subscription.md: - Aggregate root Subscription with invariants enforced in methods - Value Objects: BillingPeriod, Money (frozen) - Domain events: SubscriptionActivated, SubscriptionRenewed, SubscriptionCancelled - Repository interface SubscriptionRepository (no ORM types) - Tests covering each invariant + state transition Use frozen dataclasses (Python) or records (Java/C#). No setters on root.

checklist.check: Aggregate Root is the only entry point for mutations
No public setters on Aggregate Root or child Entities
All state transitions are named command methods with pre-condition checks
Domain Events are appended to _events list inside command methods
Events are only collected/dispatched after successful DB transaction
Cross-Aggregate references use identity (UUID), not object references
Each invariant has a corresponding failing unit test written before implementation
Domain layer has no framework imports (no Django, JPA, EF)
